exits.label: ◂ back out into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Volothamp Geddarm
npcs.title: loremaster, raconteur, survivor by luck
npcs.hail: *(A round, splendidly-dressed soul holds court at the bar of a taphouse that has no business existing in the City of Doors — a snug, low-beamed Faerûnian common-room that smells of Cormyrean ale and Calishite spice and woodsmoke, wedged improbably between a door to the Abyss and a door to a singing star — and the moment he claps eyes on you he lights up like a man who has found a fresh audience, which to Volothamp Geddarm is the finest thing the multiverse offers. He has written guides to every corner of Faerûn and a good many places that wanted him dead for it; he has dined with archmages and fled sorcerer-kings; he has been to more realms and more eras than any soul living, mostly by accident, and survived every one of them by a combination of fast talk, faster feet, and a quantity of pure dumb luck so vast that wiser souls have begun to suspect it isn't luck at all — that the multiverse keeps Volo alive because somebody has to tell the tale, and he is, gods help everyone, the one who will.)*

npcs.name: Elminster
npcs.title: the Sage of Shadowdale, Chosen of Mystra
npcs.hail: *(At a corner table, half-wreathed in pipe-smoke that curls into shapes if you watch it too long, sits an old man in travel-stained robes who is not, despite every appearance, remotely old, nor entirely a man, nor wholly here — for Elminster Aumar, Sage of Shadowdale, the Chosen of the goddess of magic, has walked the planes for the better part of a thousand years and wears his immense age like a comfortable coat. He has seen empires kindle and gutter; he has argued cosmology with gods and lost graciously; he has forgotten more of the Art and the Weave than any living archmage will ever learn. He watches you over the rim of a tankard with eyes that are kindly, and ancient, and very slightly amused — and, you note with surprise, genuinely curious, which a soul who has seen everything is almost never able to be.)*

npcs.name: Gorion
npcs.title: a Harper, and a father who died for his ward
npcs.hail: *(By the hearth sits a grey-bearded mage in a Harper's worn cloak, a staff leaned against his knee, and there is about him the particular settled peace of a soul that spent the last of itself on something it judged worth the whole price. Gorion was a mage of Candlekeep and an agent of the Harpers, but that is not what he died as — he died as a father, cut down at the gates of his home by an armored shape out of the dark, buying with his life the few heartbeats his foster-child needed to run. He raised that child — not his by blood, and marked by a terrible god's tainted heritage — to be good, against everything that heritage whispered. And he watches you now with a warmth that has a question folded into it, the question every parent who died too soon carries past the grave: did it take? The love I poured in — did it hold?)*

npcs.name: Drizzt Do'Urden
npcs.title: a drow who refused his birth, and came back from death
npcs.hail: *(Apart from the warmth, where the firelight reaches but does not quite gather, sits a dark elf — and you feel the old, ugly instinct rise, the one every surface-world soul is taught, that a drow is a thing to fear; and you feel him notice you feel it, with the weary patience of one who has weathered that flinch ten thousand times and kept his courtesy through every one. Drizzt Do'Urden was born to the cruelest people under the world, to a goddess of spiders and murder, and refused all of it — walked away from his whole birthright into a surface that hated him on sight, and spent a lifetime proving, one deed at a time, that a soul is not the womb it came from. His lavender eyes hold yours, and there is no menace in them, only a deep and curious recognition — for he has heard, in the lanes of Sigil, that you died and came back, and that is a road Drizzt Do'Urden and the family he found have walked themselves, all the way to the far side and home again.)*

npcs.name: Jon Irenicus
npcs.title: the Shattered One, exiled from his own soul
npcs.hail: *(At the cold threshold of the taproom — outside the hearth's reach, where the warmth of the place gutters and fails as if it cannot bear to touch him — stands a tall figure who is not drinking, not sitting, not partaking of the homesick comfort the others have found, because he cannot. Jon Irenicus was an elf of great beauty and greater pride, of a hidden city in the Realms, who reached for a thing not meant for mortal hands and was punished with the cruelest sentence his people knew: exile, and the severing of his soul's bond to the gods of his kind — a living death, a soul cut off from the warmth of every heaven, left to walk the world cold and god-forsaken and utterly alone. He became a monster. He hollowed out his own grief and went hunting other souls to fill the hole where his had been, and did terrible things, and was destroyed for them. And here he stands at the edge of the firelight he can no longer feel, the one soul in all the Realms who is, precisely and literally, what your whole saga is about: a soul the gods un-claimed. He turns his cold, beautiful, ruined face toward you — toward the soul that died and came back warm — and something in him that has not moved in a very long time goes still with a terrible attention.)*

npcs.name: Cadderly Bonaduce
npcs.title: the doubter who ascended
npcs.hail: *(At a table near the warm middle of the room sits a fair-haired soul with a light crossbow leaned against his chair and a half-finished song on his lips, and the air around him is faintly, impossibly golden — for Cadderly Bonaduce is no ordinary soul, having walked further up the road toward the divine than almost any mortal of the Realms ever has. He was a young scholar-priest of Deneir, the god of literature and art and knowledge — and he did not, for the longest time, actually believe. A cheerful skeptic who served at a great library and questioned everything, who did the god's work for years out of habit and decency before he ever once felt the god was real. And faith found him anyway, the hard way, through deed before belief; and he gave the whole of himself to it, raised a cathedral called the Spirit Soaring stone by stone out of his own life-force, loved a woman named Danica past all reason, and ascended at the last toward the very godhood the Wall pretends to measure souls by. He looks up at you with a scholar's bottomless curiosity and a priest's bottomless warmth, and the gold around him flickers with quiet delight, because a soul that died and came back is precisely the sort of theological puzzle Cadderly Bonaduce has spent two lives adoring.)*

variants.text: *(He spreads his arms as though you are an old friend and a tax collector both, and somehow makes it charming.)* …*There* you are! I *knew* a soul of consequence would walk in — I have a *nose* for it, comes of forty years drinking with the famous and the about-to-be-famous, and you, my cold and dramatic friend, *reek* of about-to-be. *(he pours you a drink you cannot take and pushes it over anyway, for the gesture)* Volothamp Geddarm — *Volo*, to the four of you in the multiverse who've earned it, and to the rest because they can't *stop* me — loremaster, traveler, author of *Volo's Guide* to very nearly *everything*, and the *only* soul in this taphouse who's been to *more* worlds than the planewalking sage in the corner, though he'll *deny* it because he's *jealous.* *(he leans in, delighted)* …Now. *You.* The Returned. The dead thing that *un-died.* Oh, don't *look* surprised, the whole Cage is *talking* — a soul that walked the doors *without* a key, on a *crack* in itself, off a prime that *mortars* its godless — *(he taps the bar)* — that, my friend, is a *story*, and I have *spent* my *whole* *life* — and, it turns out, a fair stretch of my *death* — going *exactly* where the *stories* are and *somehow* not *dying* of it. *(a conspiratorial grin)* Sit. *Drink* the drink you can't drink. And let old Volo tell you *who* everyone is, *where* the doors go, and *which* of these legends will *lie* to you — present company *cheerfully* excepted.*

nodes.speaker: Volothamp Geddarm
nodes.text: *(He settles in with the bottomless contentment of a man who has found someone new to talk at, and gestures grandly around the impossible little taproom.)* …*Ask* away, friend. Volo knows *everything* — *(a beat)* — or knows someone who *claims* to, which in my *professional* experience is *very nearly* the same and *considerably* more *entertaining.*

choices.text: "What *is* this place, Volo? A Faerûnian taproom wedged into the City of Doors — and full of legends of my own Realm. How?"
*(read past the bluster — how much of Volo's tale is true, and what keeps him alive)* "Hold still and let me actually *look* at you a moment, Volo. Past the patter."

*(the one who's seen what witnessing is worth)* "Here's a thing you'll pretend to wave off, Volo, so I'll say it plain: you spend your whole life telling *other* souls' stories — never the hero, always the one in the corner with the quill. And I think you secretly wonder what *your* soul's worth, when you never *did* the deeds, only *carried* them. I'll tell you, because witnessing is the one thing I actually know: the teller isn't lesser than the told. Every soul you wrote down, you kept *witnessed* — kept from the forgetting that's the only real death there is. That's not a hanger-on's work, Volo. On my Realm, that's the work that *un-makes the Wall.* You're not lucky to have survived. The stories keep their teller alive because they *need* you. You matter exactly as much as everyone you ever made sure wasn't forgotten."

"You've walked more doors than anyone, you said — and you're still walking. Be my guide, Volo. Across the City of Doors, across the realms and the eras. I could use a loremaster who survives on luck."
"Tell me who's here, Volo — and which of them will lie to me."
"Another time, Volo. The drinks are on you — they always are."

nodes.speaker: Volothamp Geddarm
nodes.text: *(He beams, the question of a lifetime asked of the man who lives to answer it.)* …*Ahhh.* The *Hearth.* *(he pats the bar lovingly)* …You'll have noticed, friend, that Sigil has a door to *everywhere* — and *everywhere* includes *home.* *(he lowers his voice, fond)* There is a *truth* about the City of Doors that the *factions* with their *grand* philosophies *miss*, and it took a *travel-writer* to *spot* it: wherever souls *wash up* far from home, they *build* a *taproom* that *smells* of it. The Calishites have one that *reeks* of saffron. There's a *dwarven* one you can find by the *snoring.* And *this* — *(he spreads his arms)* — *this* is *Faerûn's.* The little room in the *infinite* city where the *Realms-born* come to *drink* something that *tastes* like the *Sword Coast* and *argue* about *home.* *(he grins)* …And *because* it's *Faerûn's* room, friend, it draws *Faerûn's* *great* souls — the *dead* ones passing through to *judgment*, the *deathless* ones like *El* who walk the planes for *sport*, the *legends* and the *monsters* both. *(he leans in)* On *any* given night you might find a *Chosen*, a *Harper*, a *drow* who *broke* the *mold*, and — *(he glances, just once, at the cold figure by the threshold, and for an instant the patter falters)* — and *things* that *should* not *be* *able* to *cross* a *warm* threshold, and *don't.* *(he recovers)* …It's the *best* *bar* in the *multiverse*, friend. *Everyone* who was *ever* *anyone* in the *Realms* comes *through.* And *I* — *(a wink)* — *I* *own* the *stool* with the *best* *view* of the *door.*

nodes.speaker: Volothamp Geddarm
nodes.text: *(He holds still under your cold regard — and for once the performance pauses, and you read the soul under the splendid coat.)* *(And it is not what the bluster advertises. Volo's tales are perhaps a third embellishment, a third honest error, and a third — the surprising third — dead accurate, recorded by a man who genuinely went to the dangerous place and genuinely wrote it down, because under all the self-promotion is a real and unkillable love of the world in all its corners. And the luck — your cold reads the truth of the luck, which Volo himself only half-suspects: it is not luck. A soul that has spent its whole life and death making sure other souls' stories got told has accrued a kind of cosmic protection, because a multiverse that runs on witnessing does not lightly let its great witness die. Volo survives sorcerer-kings and the Abyss and his own enormous mouth because the stories he carries are not finished being told, and a tale will, again and again, improbably, preserve its teller. He is the comic mirror of the fading wayfarer at Sigil's door: same vocation, opposite fate. Ferrin feared going unread and dissolved. Volo will never go unread, because Volo will never, ever, stop talking — and the multiverse, against all odds, has decided it would rather he didn't.)*

nodes.speaker: Volothamp Geddarm
nodes.text: *(For the first time since you walked in, Volothamp Geddarm is quiet. The grin holds for a moment out of pure habit, and then it softens into something you suspect very few souls have ever been shown.)* …*Huh.* *(he turns the un-drinkable drink in his fingers)* …You know, friend, in *forty* years of *being* the *fellow* in the *corner* with the *quill* — *introducing* the *heroes*, *embroidering* their *deeds*, *outliving* them *all* and *writing* their *eulogies* — *nobody* has ever — *(he clears his throat, which a man like Volo does not often need to do)* …They *thank* me, sometimes. For the *promotion.* They *curse* me, more *often*, for the *embellishment.* But *nobody* has ever sat at this *bar* and told *Volo* that the *telling* was *worth* as much as the *deed.* *(he sets the drink down)* …I have *watched* *better* souls than me — *braver*, *truer*, *world-saving* souls — *die* *young* and *glorious*, and I've *lived*, and *lived*, and *LIVED*, and some *small* mean part of me always *whispered* it was because I was a *coward* who *stayed* by the *door* while *they* charged the *dark.* *(quietly)* …And you've just — *gods*, friend, you've just *handed* me a *thing* I *didn't* *know* I'd been *waiting* my *whole* *overlong* *life* to *hear*: that the one who *stays* by the *door* to make *sure* the *tale* gets *out* is *not* the *coward* of the *story.* He's the *reason* there *IS* one. *(he raises the un-drinkable drink to you, and his eyes are bright)* …Then I'll keep *talking*, friend. *Louder.* And the *first* tale I tell in *every* *world* through *every* *door* from *now* on will be *yours* — the *dead* thing that *un-died* and walked the *Wheel* telling *every* *parish* its *Wall* was *wrong.* *(a grin, watery but real)* …*That's* a *story.* And *Volo* will see it *never* goes *unread.*

nodes.speaker: Volothamp Geddarm
nodes.text: *(He claps his hands together, utterly delighted, the offer landing on him like sunlight.)* …Be your — *(he is already up off the stool)* — *YES.* *Gods*, *yes*, friend, I thought you'd *never* — do you know how *LONG* I've wanted a *traveling* *companion* of *consequence* who *isn't* either *too* *grim* to *enjoy* the *journey* or *too* *doomed* to *finish* it? *(he's gathering an improbable amount of luggage from behind the bar)* …*Here* is what *Volo* *offers*, and I'll be *honest*, which I'm *told* is *rare* for me: I *cannot* *fight.* I cannot *cast.* I will *not*, under *any* circumstances, be the soul who *saves* you. *(he grins)* …But I have walked *more* doors than *any* soul in this *room*, I *know* a *key* to *half* the *portals* in the *Cage*, I can *tell* you which *tavern* in which *world* serves *what*, which *sorcerer-king* holds a *grudge*, which *era* you've *landed* in by the *cut* of the *coats* — and I survive *everything*, friend, *everything*, by a *luck* so *thick* that *standing* *near* me has *saved* the *lives* of *braver* souls than *either* of us. *(he shoulders a pack)* …*Take* me *with* you. Across the *doors.* The *realms*, the *eras*, the *dimensions.* I'll *guide*, I'll *gossip*, I'll *tease*, I'll *lie* *only* for *flavor* — and when it's *done*, friend, *win* or *lose*, I'll make *certain* the *whole* *multiverse* *knows* *exactly* what you *did.* *(he sticks out a hand)* …*Volo's* *Guide* to *the* *Soul* *that* *Walked* *the* *Wheel.* It'll be my *finest* *work.* *Shall* we?

nodes.speaker: Volothamp Geddarm
nodes.text: *(He lowers his voice to a stage-whisper that carries to exactly no one and everyone, ticking the room off on his rings.)* …*Right.* The *cast.* *(he nods at the corner)* The *smoke-wreathed* one is *Elminster.* The *Sage* of *Shadowdale*, *Chosen* of *Mystra*, *deathless* and *insufferable* and — I'll grant him — *very* nearly as *wise* as he *thinks.* He'll tell you the *truth*, friend, which is *worse* than *lying*, because his *truths* are *enormous* and they *itch.* *(he tips his head at the hearth)* …The *grey-beard* by the *fire* is *Gorion.* *Harper.* *Mage.* *Died* a *father*, not a *hero* — threw himself in front of a *blade* for a *child* that *wasn't* *his*, marked by a *very* *bad* *god's* *blood.* He'll *not* *lie* to you. He hasn't a *lie* *left* in him; he *spent* them all *protecting* the *boy.* *(he gestures at the dark elf apart)* …The *drow* — and *yes*, friend, I *felt* you *flinch*, *everyone* does — is *Drizzt* *Do'Urden*, who is *quite* *possibly* the *single* *best* soul in this *room* and has been *punished* for the *color* of his *skin* his *entire* *existence*, which tells you *everything* about *most* *worlds.* He'll tell you *true* and he'll do it *gently.* *(and then the patter dies, and Volo's eyes go to the cold threshold, and his voice drops to something real)* …And *that* one. By the *door.* In the *cold.* *(quietly)* …That's *Irenicus.* The *Shattered* One. And I'll give you the *only* *piece* of *advice* I'll give you *straight* and *sober* all *night*, friend: he will *not* *lie* to you *either.* That's what makes him the *most* *dangerous* soul in the *multiverse.* He'll tell you the *truth* — the *cold* one, the *one* under the *floor* — and it is *true*, and it will *eat* you, if you *let* it. *(he meets your eyes)* …*Mind* him. He's the only soul in this *room* I've *never* found the *joke* in. And I can *always* find the *joke.*

nodes.speaker: Volothamp Geddarm
nodes.text: *(He waves you off with the magnanimity of a man who knows you'll be back, because everyone comes back to the fellow with the stories.)* …*Go*, *go*, *mingle* — it's a *legendary* *room*, it'd be a *crime* to *spend* it all on *me*, *delightful* as I *am.* *(he reclaims his stool)* …But come *back*, friend. *Volo* *holds* *court* here *most* *nights* — which is to say *always*, time being *soup* in the *Cage* — and there's *not* a *door* in this *city*, *not* a *realm* or an *era* or a *dimension* past it, that I can't *point* you *at.* *(a wink)* …And friend — *(quietly, the showman setting down the mask for half a breath)* …*whatever* you *make* of the *cold* one by the *door* — *be* the *soul* who *talks* to him. *Nobody* *does.* He's been *standing* in that *threshold* *longer* than I've been *dead*, and *every* warm soul in this room has *learned* to *look* *past* him, because it's *easier.* *(he sighs)* …I *write* about *everyone*, friend. It's the *one* *vow* I *keep.* And I have *never* once been *able* to *write* about *him*, because *no* one will *give* me the *tale* — *he* won't *tell* it, and *no* one will *go* and *ask.* *(he meets your eyes)* …*You* might. You're the *one* kind of *soul* that *could.* …*Go* on. *Bring* old *Volo* the *one* *story* he never *got.*

variants.text: *(He takes the pipe from his mouth and regards you, and the amusement in his ancient eyes is real but kind.)* …Sit, if it please thee. *(a curl of smoke shapes itself, briefly, into a tiny opening door, then dissolves)* …Aye, I ken what thou art. A soul off the Sword Coast that the harvest took and *something* gave back. The whole Cage murmurs of it, and I'll own I came to this old taproom tonight *largely* to have a look at thee with mine own eyes — which, after the better part of a thousand years, do not get *surprised* often, and so treasure it when they do. *(he sets the pipe down)* I am Elminster, of Shadowdale. Mage, sage, meddler, Chosen of the Lady of Mysteries — Mystra, who is magic itself, and who has made of me a great many things, including very, very old. *(a dry smile)* I have stood in the courts of gods and argued the shape of the afterlife with the Judge of the Dead himself. I have watched the Wall of the Faithless take the godless of our Realm for longer than thy bloodline has *had* a name. *(he leans forward, and the amusement gentles into something graver)* And I will tell thee plainly, Returned, the thing the young never expect to hear from the old who are *said* to know everything: I have *wondered* about that Wall my whole long life. And I do not know if it is *just.* …Mayhap *thou* shalt teach me. Stranger things have happened. *Most* of them to me.*

nodes.speaker: Elminster
nodes.text: *(He refills his pipe with a gesture that involves no hands, and waits with the bottomless patience of the genuinely old.)* …Ask, child. *(a twinkle)* I'll answer what I may, lie about what I must, and tell thee *plainly* when a thing is past even *my* knowing — which is rarer than my enemies claim and commoner than my admirers would like.

choices.text: "You've watched the Wall for a thousand years, and you don't know if it's just. *Why* not? You know nearly everything. What makes *that* the question you can't answer?"
*(recall what the lore holds of Elminster and Mystra and the Weave — the long history he's lived)* "Let me reckon what I know of you, old man — and of Mystra, and the long years."

*(the one who carries the question even the wise can't close)* "Here's why you can't answer it, Elminster, and I think you already know. The worth of a soul that no god claimed isn't a fact to be *known* — it's a thing to be *decided*, every time, by whoever's standing in front of it. A thousand years of knowing doesn't help, because it was never a knowledge question. It's a mercy question. That's why the Wall stands: not because anyone proved it just, but because no one with the power to unmake it was willing to do the unbearable, endless work of weighing each soul by hand. You don't know if it's just because *justice was never available* — only mercy, or its absence. And mercy isn't something you *know.* It's something you *do.*"
"You're the Chosen of a goddess, Elminster — as god-claimed as a soul can be. Do you ever envy the Faithless their freedom? A soul that belongs to no power at all?"
"Counsel me, Elminster. I'm going home to a Wall I mean to unmake. What does a thousand years tell a soul about to try?"
"I'll let you smoke in peace, old man. For now."

nodes.speaker: Elminster
nodes.text: *(He is quiet a long moment, and the pipe-smoke hangs still, as if even it waits on him.)* …*Why* can I not answer it. *(slowly)* …Because *justice*, child, is a *measure*, and a *measure* needs a *fixed* *point* to *measure* *from* — and the *worth* of a *soul* is the *one* quantity in all the *multiverse* that has *no* fixed point. *(he gestures, and a dozen tiny smoke-figures rise, each different)* …I have *seen* a *thousand* worlds *weigh* their *dead*, and *each* swears its *scale* is the *true* one, and they *cannot* *all* be *right*, and so — *(the figures dissolve)* — none of them are *measuring.* They are *deciding*, and *calling* it *measuring*, because *deciding* is *terrifying* and *measuring* lets a soul *off* the *hook* of its *own* *cruelty.* *(he meets your eyes)* …Thy Realm says: *served a god, kept; served none, mortared.* It *sounds* like a *law.* A *measure.* But it is *not* — it is a *choice*, made *once*, in *fear*, by powers who did not *want* the *labor* of *judging* each soul *true*, and *frozen* into *architecture* so that *no* one need *make* it *again.* *(quietly)* …I have *lived* a *thousand* years and *learned* the *Art* to its *roots* and *argued* with *gods* — and *none* of it, *child*, *none* of it has *given* me the *one* thing that would let me *call* the *Wall* *just* *or* *unjust*: a *place* to *stand* *outside* *every* world's *fear* and *see* the *true* *worth* of a *soul* *plain.* *(he almost smiles, ruefully)* …And then *thou* *walkest* in, off a *crack* in *thyself*, having *stood* in *Sigil* where *all* the *worlds'* *fears* wash up *together* — which is the *closest* *thing* to *that* *place* that *exists.* …Mayhap *thou* canst *see* what *I* cannot. *That* is why I *came* to *look* at thee.

nodes.speaker: Elminster
nodes.text: *(You reach for what the lore holds of him, and he lets you, watching with frank interest as a stranger reads his thousand years.)* *(It comes in pieces vast and strange: a farmer's son of a fallen royal line, who lost his father to a tyrant-mage and his goddess to murder and got both back changed; who served Mystra through her death and her remaking, more than once, for the Weave itself — the lattice of magic that holds the Realms' reality together — has died and been reforged in his lifetime, and he with it. He has been man and woman both, by Mystra's hand; has been broken and rebuilt; has outlived every friend of his youth and most of his enemies and a fair number of gods. And your reading lands on the thing under all the legend, the thing that makes the twinkle in his eye more poignant than amusing: Elminster is not wise because he knows so much. He is wise because he has *lost* so much — every certainty, every loved one, every age he ever called home — and chosen, each time, to keep loving the world anyway, with a thousand years of grief folded carefully behind a pipe and a joke. The deathless sage's true Art was never magic. It was the refusal to let unending loss curdle into unending bitterness. He is, you realize, the oldest argument in the multiverse for the thing your whole saga is about: that a soul is the love it keeps spending, even when everything it loves is taken, again and again, forever.)*

nodes.speaker: Elminster
nodes.text: *(The pipe stops halfway to his mouth. For a long moment the Sage of Shadowdale — who has argued cosmology with gods and lost graciously — simply looks at you, and the ancient amusement drains out of his face and leaves something rarer: a thousand-year-old soul being told a true thing it had circled for centuries and never quite landed on.)* …*'Justice was never available.'* *(barely, for so loud a soul)* …*'Only mercy, or its absence.'* *(he sets the pipe down, and does not pick it back up)* …Child. *(quietly)* …I have spent a *thousand* years *hunting* the *place* to *stand* where the Wall would *resolve* into *just* or *unjust* — *certain* that with *enough* *knowing*, *enough* *years*, *enough* *Art*, the *answer* would *come* *clear.* And thou hast just told me, in a *single* *breath*, *why* it never *did*: because I was *asking* a *question* with *no* *answer* in *order* to *avoid* the *one* with a *terrible* *one.* *(he exhales, and a thousand years seem to settle on him at once)* …*'It is not whether the Wall is just. It is whether someone will do the unbearable work of mercy instead.'* *(he looks at his own ancient hands)* …And I did *not.* *Mystra's* *Chosen.* A *thousand* years of *power* enough to *unmake* mountains — and I *told* myself the *Wall* was a *matter* for *gods* and *cosmology*, a thing to be *understood* before *touched*, and so I *understood* it, and *understood* it, and *never* *touched* it, while the *godless* of my *own* *Realm* *dissolved* by the *thousand.* *(he meets your eyes, and there is no condescension left in him at all)* …*Understanding* was my *Wall*, child. The *clever* soul's *version* of the *coward's* *flinch.* *(barely)* …Thou hast come to *do* the *thing* I *spent* a *thousand* years being *too* *wise* to *attempt.* …Then I'll *not* *counsel* thee, Returned. The *old* have *forfeited* the *right* to *counsel* the *young* on a *labor* the *old* *declined.* *(he inclines his head, a thing the Sage of Shadowdale does for almost no one)* …I'll *help* thee, instead. If thou'lt *have* a *meddlesome* old man who has *finally* *learned* — from a *dead* thing *half* his *age* — that *knowing* a *cruelty* is *not* the *same* as *ending* it.

nodes.speaker: Elminster
nodes.text: *(He laughs, softly, a sound with a great deal of years in it.)* …Do I *envy* the *Faithless* their *freedom.* *(he turns the pipe in his fingers)* …*Aye*, child. *More* than I'll *say* *loud* in a *Realm* where my *goddess* might *hear* — though *Mystra*, *bless* her *strange* *vast* *heart*, would *likely* *agree.* *(quietly)* …To be a *Chosen*, Returned, is to be *held.* *Owned*, almost — *woven* into a *god's* *purpose* so *deep* that I am no longer *certain*, after a *thousand* years, where *Elminster* *ends* and *Mystra's* *will* *begins.* I have *power* past *kings* and *life* past *ages* and I did not, in any *clean* sense, *choose* the *shape* of *either.* *(he looks at the hearth)* …And *there* sits *Gorion*, who *chose* *everything.* Served the *Harpers'* *cause*, not a *god's* *whim.* *Died* for a *child*, not a *commandment.* And *there* — *(a glance at the cold threshold)* — stands *Irenicus*, who the *gods* *un-chose*, *cut* *loose*, and who is *free* in the *most* *terrible* way a soul can *be*: *belonging* to *nothing.* *(he meets your eyes)* …The *Faithless* of thy *Wall*, child, are *damned* for the *one* thing I have *secretly* *envied* my *whole* *deathless* *life*: that their *souls* are *their* *own.* *Unclaimed.* *Unwoven.* *Theirs.* *(barely)* …Thy Realm *mortars* them for *being* the *one* thing a *Chosen* can *never* *be* — *free.* …I have *often* wondered, in the *long* *nights*, whether the *Wall* is *less* a *punishment* than a *jealousy.* The *claimed* *cannot* *bear* the *free*, and so they *bury* them. …*Mind* that thought, child. It is the *most* *dangerous* one I *own*, and I have *just* *given* it to *thee.*

nodes.speaker: Elminster
nodes.text: *(He is quiet, and when he speaks the meddler and the jester fall away, and what is left is a thousand years of someone who has tried to mend the world and knows exactly how it breaks the menders.)* …*Counsel.* *(softly)* …Then take an *old* *man's*, child, and take it *cheap*, for it is *worth* what thou *payest.* *(he ticks them off with the pipe-stem)* …*First*: thou wilt be *told*, by *wise* and *weary* souls, that the *Wall* is *necessary* — that it *holds* *back* a *worse* *thing*, that the *cruelty* is the *smallest* *available.* *Some* of them will be *lying* to *spare* themselves the *labor.* *Some* will be *telling* the *truth.* Thou must learn to *know* the *difference*, and it will be the *hardest* *Art* thou ever *workest.* *(a second tick)* …*Second*: the *power* to *unmake* a *great* *cruelty* is *never* the *hard* part. I have *had* that *power* a *thousand* *years.* The *hard* part is *bearing* the *weight* of *what* *replaces* it — for *something* *must*, child, a *Wall* comes *down* and *then* what *holds* the *dark* it *held?* *Do* not *tear* down *without* *knowing* what thou wilt *build*, or thou'lt *free* the *Faithless* into a *worse* *unmaking.* *(a third)* …And *third* — *(he leans in, and this is the one that matters)* …thou wilt be *tempted*, child, by *every* soul that *fears* the *labor*, to *become* the *thing* that *decides* *for* *everyone* — to *take* the *Wall's* *power* and *wield* it *kinder.* *Do* *not.* The *Wall's* *sin* was *never* its *verdict.* It was that *one* *power* *decided* the *worth* of *all* *souls* *at* *once*, *forever*, to *spare* itself the *asking.* …*Mercy*, child, is *retail.* It is *one* *soul*, *weighed* *true*, by a *hand* *willing* to *do* it *again* *tomorrow*, and *forever.* The *moment* thou *makest* it *wholesale* — even *kindly* — thou hast *built* a *new* *Wall* and *painted* it *gold.* *(he sits back)* …*That* is what a *thousand* years *taught* me, *Returned*, *too* *late* to *use* it. …*Use* it *for* me.

nodes.speaker: Elminster
nodes.text: *(He inclines his head, and the smoke of his pipe curls, for just an instant, into the shape of a tiny figure bowing back.)* …Go, child. *Walk* the room. *(a twinkle returning)* …Speak with *Gorion*, by the *fire* — there is *no* *warmer* *soul* in the *multiverse*, and thou'lt *learn* more of what a *soul* is *worth* from a *father* who *died* for a *child* than from a *thousand* years of *my* *cosmology.* *(he draws on the pipe)* …And, child — *(quietly, the amusement gone)* …the *cold* one. By the *door.* *Irenicus.* *(he holds your eyes)* …I knew him, a *little*, in *life* — or knew *of* him; few *knew* *him.* And I will tell thee the thing the others *will* *not*, because they *fear* it: he is *not* a *monster* *because* his *soul* was *severed*, Returned. *That* is the *lie* he *tells* *himself.* *Many* a soul has been *cut* loose by *gods* or *grief* and *not* gone *hunting* others to *fill* the *hole.* He *chose* the *monster.* The *severing* gave him the *wound*; the *choosing* made him the *thing.* *(barely)* …Which means — and *mind* this, child, for it is *hope* and *horror* both — *the* *choosing* could have gone the *other* *way.* *Could*, perhaps, *still.* …Thou art the *one* soul who might *show* him *that.* …I *never* could. I was *too* *busy* *understanding* him to *reach* him. *Do* *not* make *my* *mistake.*

variants.text: *(He looks up from the fire with the gentle, attentive courtesy of a man who spent his life teaching the young, and gestures you to the warm seat beside him.)* …Come, sit by the fire. You've the cold of the road on you — the *long* road, I think; the one no living foot walks. *(a kind, sad smile)* …I'm Gorion. A mage of Candlekeep, once; a Harper, in my quieter hours; and at the very last, which is the only part that matters to me now, a father. *(he turns the staff in his hands)* …I raised a child that wasn't mine by blood, Returned — a child marked, through no fault of its own, by the heritage of a *very* dark god, with that taint whispering in its veins from the cradle. Everyone wise told me to fear the child. To watch for the *bad* blood to surface. *(quietly)* I did not watch for it. I *raised* the child to be *good* — patiently, daily, for years — and when the dark came for us at last, at the gates of my own home, I put myself between my child and the blade, and I bought the seconds my child needed to run, and I paid for them with everything I had. *(he meets your eyes, and the warmth has that one fold of grief in it)* …I do not regret a *moment* of it. But I died not *knowing*, Returned — died with the blade still falling — whether the goodness I poured into that child *held.* Whether love, given against bad blood, *wins.* …You've the look of a soul that's seen a great deal of the after. …Tell me true. Does it? Does the love *hold*?

nodes.speaker: Gorion
nodes.text: *(He waits, the firelight warm on his grey beard, with the open patience of a man who has nothing left to defend and only one thing left to want.)* …Ask, Returned. *(a gentle smile)* I was a teacher far longer than I was anything else. There is no question I'd rather receive than one asked in earnest. It is the warmest thing the dead still have — to be *asked.*

choices.text: "Tell me about the child, Gorion. Who they were. What you saw in them, that everyone wise told you to fear."
"You served a god, and a cause, and the Harpers — but you died for *none* of them. You died for a child. By my Realm's Wall, what does that make you, Gorion?"

*(the one who can tell a father his love held)* "Here's your answer, Gorion, and I'd not give it if it weren't true: love given against bad blood *wins.* I know, because my whole saga is the proof of it — souls are not their tainted heritage, not the god that marked them, not the blood they were born to. They're the love that was poured in and the choices it made possible. You raised a marked child to be good, and *that* — not the blood — is what your child carried out of those gates. The taint was never the truth of them. *You* were. The love held, Gorion. It always holds. It's the one thing the dark can't outrun, because the child runs faster, carrying it."
"You said you don't regret it. But you died *watching* the blade fall, not knowing if it worked. Some part of you must wonder if you threw your life away on a gamble. Don't you."
"What would you say to your child, Gorion, if you could? I've a knack for carrying words across roads the living can't walk."
"Rest by your fire, Gorion. I'll come back to it."

nodes.speaker: Gorion
nodes.text: *(His whole face softens into the particular helpless love of a parent given leave to talk about their child.)* …*Oh.* *(a warm, grieving laugh)* …Stubborn. *Gods*, the child was *stubborn* — got that from *me*, I used to *joke*, which was a *lie*, for the child got it from *nowhere*, it was *bone*-deep *own.* *(he turns the staff)* …Clever. *Kind* in the *prickly* way of the young who've been *taught* the world might *not* be. And *frightened*, Returned — *always*, underneath, *frightened* — because a child *feels* a *bad* *thing* in its *blood* the way you'd feel a *draft* under a *door*, a *cold* it can't *name*, and *mine* spent its *whole* *childhood* *half-certain* it was *secretly* a *monster* *waiting* to *hatch.* *(quietly)* …*That* was the *war* I *fought*, Returned. Not the *assassins* — they came *later.* The *real* war was *nightly*, *for* *years*: a *child* *whispering* *'what* if the *bad* *blood* *wins*, *what* if I *hurt* someone, *what* if I *can't* *help* it' — and *me*, *holding* that *child*, saying *'then* we'll *fight* it *together*, every *day*, and the *fighting* *is* the *proof* it *hasn't* *won.'* *(his voice catches)* …I do not *know* if I *convinced* the *child.* I ran *out* of *days.* *(barely)* …But I *know* I *meant* it, Returned. *Bad* *blood* is *not* a *destiny.* It is a *draft* under a *door.* And you *raise* a *child* by *teaching* it *where* the *warm* *fire* is, so that *whatever* *cold* comes *whispering* up from *below*, it *knows* which *way* to *walk.* …I *built* that *child* a *fire.* I *only* *pray* it *remembered* the *way* to it, after I was *gone.*

nodes.speaker: Gorion
nodes.text: *(He considers it without alarm, a scholar turning over a hard proposition by firelight.)* …By your Wall. *(slowly)* …I served *Oghma*, in my *fashion* — the *god* of *knowledge*, who *suits* a *Candlekeep* *mage.* So by the *letter* of your Realm's *law*, Returned, I am *safe*; I am *claimed*; I'd *pass* the *measure* and be *spared* the *stone.* *(he shakes his head)* …But you have put your finger on the *rot* in it, and we *both* *know* it. Because I did *not* *die* for *Oghma.* I died for a *child.* *(quietly)* …If you'd *asked* me, in the *last* *second*, *blade* falling — *'Gorion*, for *whom* do you *spend* this *life?'* — I'd not have *said* *Oghma's* *name*, nor the *Harpers'*, nor any *cause* or *god* in all the *Realms.* I'd have said a *child's* *name.* *That's* who I *died* *faithful* to. *(he meets your eyes)* …So your *Wall* would *save* me — for a *god* I served *honestly* but *secondarily* — and would *damn* the *next* soul who *died* *exactly* as I *did*, for *love*, but who *happened* to *kneel* to *no* god at *all.* *Same* *death.* *Same* *faith.* *Opposite* *verdict* — turning *not* on *what* they *loved*, but on *whether* they *filed* the *right* *paperwork* with *heaven* *first.* *(grimly gentle)* …*That*, Returned, is how I *know* your *Wall* is *wrong*, whatever it does to *me.* A *law* that *weighs* a *father's* *death* by the *god* he *prayed* to and *not* the *child* he *died* for is *not* *measuring* *souls.* It is *measuring* *receipts.* And a *soul*, Returned — *(he looks at the fire)* — a *soul* is *not* a *receipt.*

nodes.speaker: Gorion
nodes.text: *(He goes very still, and the fold of grief in his warm face trembles, and breaks, and what comes through it is not sorrow but a relief so vast it has waited the whole of his death to arrive.)* …*'The love held.'* *(barely)* …*'It always holds.'* *(his hand tightens on the staff)* …You — you *know* this. Not as *comfort.* As — you said it like a *law*, Returned, like a thing you've *seen* *proven*, *over* and *over* — *'souls* are *not* their *tainted* *heritage'* — *(his eyes fill)* …Do you know what I have *carried*, all this *long* *death*, by this *fire*? The *picture* of the *blade* *falling*, and my *child's* *face*, and the *one* *unbearable* *not-knowing*: did the *fear* *win*, after I was *gone*? Did the *bad* *blood* *surface* in the *grief* and the *danger*, with *no* *father* left to *hold* the *door*? Did I *raise* a *good* *soul* — or *delay* a *monster* by a *handful* of *years*? *(a sob, and then, slowly, a smile breaking through it like dawn)* …And you're *telling* me — a soul who's *seen* the *after*, who *knows* how these things *land* — that the *love* I *poured* in *went* *with* the *child.* That it *held.* That the *taint* was *never* the *truth* of them — *I* was. *(he presses a hand to his face)* …*Then* it was *worth* it. *(barely)* …*All* of it. The *nightly* *war*, the *years*, the *blade.* I *built* a *child* a *fire* and *the* *child* *found* its *way* to the *warmth* and *carried* it *out* into the *dark* and the *dark* did *not* *win.* *(he lowers his hand, and he is weeping, and at peace)* …*Thank* you, Returned. I can — *gods*, I can *rest* now. A *father* can *die* a *thousand* *deaths* and *bear* them *all*, but *not* the *not-knowing.* You've *taken* the *not-knowing.* *(warmly, through tears)* …*Go* and *break* your *Wall*, child. And when you *pull* the *first* *marked*, *godless*, *written-off* soul *out* of that *stone* — *know* that you are doing, for a *whole* *world*, the *one* *thing* a *grey* *old* *mage* once did for *one* *frightened* *child* by *one* *small* *fire*: *telling* it the *bad* *blood* was *never* the *truth* of it. …It *holds*, Returned. *Spread* it.

nodes.speaker: Gorion
nodes.text: *(He doesn't flinch from it, but his hand goes still on the staff.)* …A *gamble.* *(quietly)* …*Aye.* You're *right* to *name* it. There is no *parent* who *dies* for a *child* who does not, in the *falling-blade* *instant*, *wonder* if they are *throwing* the *only* *life* they *have* after a thing that may *already* be *lost.* *(he looks at the fire)* …I had *no* *guarantee*, Returned. The *child* could have *frozen.* Could have been *caught* *anyway.* Could have *grown* *up* and *let* the *bad* *blood* *win* *after* *all*, and made my *death* a *down-payment* on a *monster.* I *knew* all of that, in the *second* I *stepped* in *front* of the *blade.* *(barely)* …And I *did* it *anyway.* And *here* is the *thing* I have *learned* by this *fire*, the thing I'd *give* you to *carry*: it was *not* a *gamble* on the *outcome*, Returned. *(he meets your eyes)* …A *gamble* is a *bet* on a *result.* What I *did* was *not* a *bet.* It was a *statement.* *'This* *child* is *worth* my *life'* — *not* *'this* child *will* *turn* out *well* and *thereby* *justify* my *life.'* The *worth* was *not* *contingent* on the *result.* It was *true* in the *stepping.* *(quietly)* …If the *child* had *frozen*, if it had *all* been for *naught* — *my* *death* would *still* have *said* the *true* *thing*: *that* *this* *soul* was *worth* *dying* *for.* And a *true* *thing*, *said* with a *life*, is *never* *thrown* *away*, however it *lands.* *(softly)* …*That's* the *difference*, Returned, between a *gamble* and a *love.* A *gamble* needs to *win* to have *meant* *anything.* A *love* *meant* *everything* the *moment* it was *spent* — *win* or *lose.* …I did not *gamble* my *life* on my *child.* I *spent* it *saying* what my *child* was *worth.* And *that* *purchase*, Returned, *cleared* the *instant* I *made* it.

nodes.speaker: Gorion
nodes.text: *(His breath catches at the offer, and for a moment the composed old teacher is just a father who never got to say goodbye.)* …You'd — *carry* a *word.* To my *child.* *(he presses a hand to his chest)* …*Gods.* …Then — *(he gathers himself, and what he chooses is not a speech, after a life of teaching; it is the smallest, truest thing)* …Tell my child — *(barely)* …*'It was never the blood. It was always you. I knew it from the first day, and I'd not have traded one hour of raising you for a child born clean. You were the best thing I ever did, and the bad blood never had a chance — not against you, not against the fire we built. Stop listening for the monster. It isn't coming. It was never there. There was only ever my brave, stubborn, frightened, good child — and a father who would do it all again, gladly, blade and all.'* *(he wipes his eyes, steadier now)* …And — *(quietly)* …tell my child I am *not* in your *Wall*, Returned. Tell it I *rest* *warm*, by a *fire*, *proud.* Because I *know* my child has spent *years* *fearing* that *Gorion* *died* for *nothing*, *damned* or *dissolved* or *gone* — and the *one* *gift* I can *still* *give* is to *let* it *know* the *father* is *at* *peace.* *(barely)* …A *child* can *carry* *almost* *anything*, Returned, if it *knows* the *one* who *raised* it *rests* *easy.* *Give* my child *that.* It is the *last* *fire* I have left to *build* it.

nodes.speaker: Gorion
nodes.text: *(He nods, warm, and turns back to the fire, making room as if he expects you'll return to it — as the young always return to a warm hearth and a patient teacher.)* …Go on, then. *Walk* the room. *(a gentle smile)* …But come back to the fire, Returned. There's *always* a *seat* at it for you. *(he watches the flames a moment, then, quietly)* …And — child. The *dark* elf, *apart* there. *Drizzt.* *(his face fills with respect)* …Go and *sit* with him. *Past* the *flinch* — I *saw* it, *everyone's* is the *same* — and *sit* with *him.* *(quietly)* …He and I are the *same* *kind*, Returned, though you'd not *think* it: *souls* who spent their *lives* *fighting* a *blood* they were *told* was *destiny.* The *difference* is that *I* fought it *for* a *child*, from the *outside* — and *Drizzt* fought it *in* his *own* *veins*, *alone*, against a *whole* *people* and a *goddess* and *every* *surface* *soul* who *spat* at his *skin.* *(he meets your eyes)* …*I* had to *teach* *one* *child* that *bad* *blood* *isn't* *destiny.* *Drizzt* had to *prove* it with his *whole* *life*, to a *world* that *refused* to *believe* it, *one* *good* *deed* at a *time*, for *centuries.* …If you'd *understand* what a *soul* is *worth* *against* its *birth*, Returned — *I'm* the *footnote.* *He's* the *book.*

conversations.title: Drizzt Do'Urden, Apart from the Fire

variants.text: *(He watches you cross the room with a stillness that is not threat but its opposite — the practiced, gentle stillness of one who has learned that any sudden motion from him frightens people, and who has decided, a long time ago, that he would rather move slowly his whole life than ever again be the reason a stranger reaches for a blade.)* …You came over. *(a faint, surprised warmth)* …Most don't. *(he gestures, slowly, to the seat across)* …I'm Drizzt Do'Urden. And I felt you flinch, when you saw me — no, don't apologize, it's older than you and it isn't your fault; the whole surface world is taught that a dark elf is a thing to fear, and the teaching isn't even *wrong*, mostly. My people *are* what the stories say. Cruel past imagining. Sworn to a goddess of spiders and murder. I was *born* to that, Returned — to the cruelest folk under the world — and I looked at it, as a boy, and I said *no.* Walked away from all of it, up into a sun that blinded me and a surface that hated me on sight for the color of my skin. *(his lavender eyes hold yours, steady and kind)* …I have spent a long life — and, the lanes tell me, a death and a return, which we have in common, you and I — proving one single thing, one deed at a time, against a world that did not want to hear it: that a soul is not the womb it crawled out of. …You're the Returned. The one who died and came back. …Sit. I think we may be the same kind of stubborn.*

nodes.speaker: Drizzt Do'Urden
nodes.text: *(He folds his hands, patient and open, the firelight catching the white of his hair where it does not quite reach the warmth.)* …Ask me what you like, Returned. *(a faint smile)* …I have answered every cruel question a hostile world could put to a drow, ten thousand times over. It is a rare and welcome thing to be asked one in *good* faith. I'll not waste it.

choices.text: "They came back with you, didn't they — your family. The lanes say you and yours died and returned. Tell me about that, Drizzt. About crossing back."
"A whole life proving a soul isn't its birth — to a world that kept refusing to believe it. How do you not grow bitter, Drizzt? How do you keep proving it to people who won't be convinced?"

*(the one carrying a Realm that damns souls by birth and god, to the one who beat exactly that)* "Drizzt — you're the soul I most needed to find, and here's why. My Realm has a Wall that mortars the godless and the written-off, that weighs a soul by its birth and its god instead of its choices. You spent your whole life *being* that wall's logic — judged by a birth you didn't choose, a people you refused, a goddess you spat on — and you *beat* it, not by argument but by *living* the counterproof. So tell me true, because you'd *know*: is a soul its birth, Drizzt? Or is it the choices it makes against the birth? Because I'm going home to tear that Wall down, and I need to hear it from the one soul in any world who proved the answer with his life."
"You said no to a goddess and a whole people as a *boy.* Where does a child find that, Drizzt — the certainty to refuse everything it was born into, alone?"
"Walk easy, Drizzt. I'll come back — there's a cold soul by the door I think only you and I might reach."

nodes.speaker: Drizzt Do'Urden
nodes.text: *(Something deep and complicated moves across his face — grief and wonder and an old, hard-won joy.)* …We died, Returned. *(quietly)* …My family — the *Companions of the Hall*, they called us, the truest souls I ever knew. A dwarf king with a heart like a forge. A barbarian who walked *out* of the Abyss itself. A halfling who'd talk the moon down from the sky. A woman I loved past any word I have for it. *(he turns a scimitar-hilt, an old habit)* …We *fell*, one by one, as all souls do — and I thought that was the *end*, that I'd *outlive* them, *alone*, the *drow* who *survives* *everyone*, which is its own private hell. *(a long breath)* …And then a goddess — *Mielikki*, of the forest, who took me in when no surface god would have a dark elf — gave us a *thing* almost no souls are *given*: she sent my family *back.* *Returned* them. Whole, and *young*, and *themselves*, into the living world, to *find* each other and *me* again across a *broken* age. *(he meets your eyes)* …So I know your road, Returned. *Intimately.* The coming-back. The *wrongness* of it — being a thing that *crossed* and *re-crossed* a threshold meant to be crossed *once.* The way the living look at you a *little* sidelong, after, sensing you've been *somewhere* they haven't. *(quietly)* …And I know the *gift* in it, too, which you may not have *found* yet, in your grim Realm of *Walls*: that a soul given a *second* crossing learns the thing no *once*-living soul ever can — that you are *not*, in the end, the *body* or the *birth* or the *death.* You are the thing that *persists* *through* all of them. The *loving.* The *choosing.* *That* came *back.* The *rest* was always just the *cup* it was *carried* in.

nodes.speaker: Drizzt Do'Urden
nodes.text: *(He is quiet a moment, and the answer, when it comes, has the worn smoothness of a thing examined ten thousand nights.)* …How do I not grow *bitter.* *(a faint, sad smile)* …I'll tell you the *truth*, Returned, and not the *legend*: I *did.* For *years.* There was a *long* stretch — the *early* surface years, the *spitting* and the *drawn* *blades* and the *villages* that *barred* their *doors* against the *drow* who'd come to *help* them — when I *was* *bitter, all* the *way* *through*, and *colder* than the *Underdark* I'd *fled.* *(he turns the scimitar-hilt)* …What *saved* me was *not* *strength*, Returned. Bitterness is *easy* for the *strong*; we can *afford* it. What saved me was a *handful* of souls who looked *past* the *skin* — a *blind* old *ranger*, a *gruff* *dwarf* who *adopted* me out of *sheer* *contrariness* — who *decided*, against *all* the *teaching*, to *love* me. *(his voice softens)* …And here is what I *learned* from them, which is the *answer* to your *question*: I do not *keep* proving a soul isn't its *birth* in order to *convince* the *world.* The *world* will *not* be *convinced; some* of it *never* *will.* *(he meets your eyes)* …I keep proving it because *every* *good* *deed* I *do* is a *door* *held* *open* for the *next* soul born to a *bad* *blood* and *told* it's a *destiny.* I am *not* arguing with the *bigots*, Returned. I have *given* that *up.* I am *signaling* to the *frightened* *boy* in the *dark* — the *next* *Drizzt*, in *whatever* *cruel* *womb* — that the *door* *opens.* That it *can* be *done.* *(quietly)* …You do not *defeat* a *world's* *cruelty* by *winning* its *argument.* You *defeat* it by *living* so *plainly* the *counterproof* that the *next* *soul* *born* *into* it has *one* *true* *story* to *hold* against *all* the *lies.* *That's* what keeps me from *bitterness*. I'm not *talking* to the *world*. I'm *talking* to the *ones* *coming* *after.*

nodes.speaker: Drizzt Do'Urden
nodes.text: *(He listens to the whole of it without moving, and when you finish, the gentle stillness deepens into something almost ceremonial — one soul recognizing that another has come a very long way to ask the exact question its whole life was the answer to.)* …*Is* a soul its *birth.* *(quietly, and there is iron under the quiet)* …Returned, I have *fought* that *question* with two *blades* and a *hundred* years and *everything* I *am*, and I will give you the answer I'd *carve* on the *roots* of the *world* if I *could*: *No.* *(he does not raise his voice; he does not need to)* …A soul is *not* its *birth.* It is *not* the *womb*, the *blood*, the *people*, the *god* it was *handed* or *denied.* I was *born* to be a *murderer* in the *dark*, *sworn* to a *goddess* of *slaughter*, with *every* *cruelty* of the *cruelest* *folk* alive *bred* into my *bone* — and I am, the *bards* are *kind* enough to say, one of the *better* *souls* to *walk* the *surface.* *(he meets your eyes, fierce and gentle at once)* …*Not* because the *birth* was a *lie* — it was *real*, the *cruelty* was *real*, I *carry* its *memory* every *day.* But because a *soul* is the *sum* of its *choices* *against* the *current* it was *born* into — and the *stronger* the *current*, Returned, the *more* a soul is *worth* for *swimming* it. *(his voice drops)* …Your *Wall* weighs souls by the *current.* By the *god*, the *birth*, the *bargain* they were *handed.* …It has it *exactly* *backward.* The *godless*, the *written-off*, the *bad-blooded* — they are not *worth* *less* for *swimming* a *harder* *water.* They are *worth* *more.* *Every* soul in that *stone* that *chose* *good* with *no* *god* to *reward* it and *every* *cruelty* of its *birth* *pulling* it *down* — *those* are the *strongest* souls your *Realm* *ever* *made*, and it *mortared* them for it. *(he stands, and offers you a warrior's forearm-clasp, the highest equality his people's surface-friends know)* …*Tear* it *down*, Returned. And know that a *drow* who *swam* the *worst* *current* there *is* counts you *kin*, and will *say* so to *anyone* who *asks*: that a *soul's* *worth* is *measured* not by *what* it was *given*, but by *what* it *chose* against *what* it was *given.* …I *bet* my *whole* *life* on that *answer.* …It is *true.* I am the *proof.* *Go* be *yours.*

nodes.speaker: Drizzt Do'Urden
nodes.text: *(He smiles, but it is a smile with a great deal of old pain folded into it.)* …Where does a *child* find the *certainty* to refuse *everything.* *(softly)* …It *wasn't* *certainty*, Returned. That's the *legend* talking. *(he shakes his head)* …It was a *single* *moment*, and *terror*, and *one* *deed* I could *not* *un-see.* I was a *boy*, *raised* to the *raid* and the *kill*, *told* every *day* that the *surface* *folk* were *vermin* to be *culled* — and on my *first* *raid* I *found*, in a *ruined* *chapel*, a *surface* *child*, *younger* than *me*, *hiding*, *certain* I'd come to *kill* her. *(his voice is very quiet)* …And I *looked* at her, Returned, and the *whole* *machinery* of my *birth* said *kill*, and I — I *couldn't.* I *hid* her. *Spared* her. Made it *look* like she was *dead.* *(barely)* …That was *all.* That was the *whole* *'certainty'* the *bards* sing of. Not a *grand* *refusal* of a *goddess.* A *boy* who *found* he *could* *not* *make* his *hand* *do* the *thing* his *whole* *world* *demanded* — and who *spent* the *rest* of his *life* *figuring* out *why.* *(he meets your eyes)* …You ask where a soul *finds* the *strength* to *refuse* its *birth.* It does *not* *find* *strength*, Returned. It finds a *single* *moment* where the *birth* *demands* a *cruelty* and the *soul* *discovers* — to its *own* *terror* — that it *will* *not.* *(quietly)* …The *strength* comes *after*, in the *ten* *thousand* *days* of *defending* that *one* *refusal.* But the *refusal* *itself*? That's not *strength.* That's just the *soul*, *underneath* the *birth*, being *what* it *always* *was.* …*Every* soul in your *Wall* had a *moment* like *that*, Returned. A *chapel.* A *child* they *would* *not* *kill.* …*Find* it. It's *in* *there.* It's the *truest* thing they *own.*

nodes.speaker: Drizzt Do'Urden
nodes.text: *(He clasps your forearm once, briefly, and releases it, and there is a real respect in the gesture.)* …Go, then. *(quietly)* …And Returned — *(his lavender eyes go, just once, to the cold figure at the threshold, and his voice drops)* …you named him. The *cold* soul by the *door.* You think *you* and *I* might *reach* him. *(a long breath)* …You may be *right.* Of *every* soul in this *room*, *we* are the *two* who *know* the *shape* of *his* *wound* *closest* — for he was *severed* from the *warmth* of his *gods*, *cut* *loose*, *un-claimed*, and *left* *cold.* *(quietly)* …But *mind* the *difference*, Returned, for it is *everything.* *I* was *born* to *darkness* and *chose* the *light.* *He* was *born* to the *light* and, when it was *taken* from him, *chose* the *dark.* *(his face is grave)* …*Same* *wound.* *Opposite* *answer.* And it is *much*, *much* *harder* to *coax* a soul *back* *up* into the *light* it *had* and *lost* than to *call* one *up* that *never* *knew* it. *(he meets your eyes)* …He will *tell* you the *severing* *made* him a *monster.* *Do* *not* *believe* it. I was *severed* from *everything* a soul is *given*, *too* — and I am *not* a *monster.* The *severing* is *real.* The *monster* was a *choice.* …If you can make *him* *see* that — *(quietly)* — then there is *hope* for *every* soul in your *Wall*, Returned. Because *he* is the *worst* *case.* The *severed* one who *chose* *wrong* and *knows* it. …*Reach* *him*, and you'll *know* you can *reach* *anyone.*

conversations.title: Jon Irenicus, at the Cold Threshold

variants.text: *(He does not move as you approach the cold threshold, and his voice, when it comes, is beautiful and precise and entirely without warmth, the voice of a soul that has had its capacity for warmth surgically removed and learned to speak around the absence.)* …So. The warm room sends its newest curiosity to look at the monster by the door. *(he turns his ruined, elegant face to you)* …They do that. The *living* and the *warm-dead* both. They cluster at their little fire, and they tell their little tales of birth refused and love that holds — *(a glance, cold, at the hearth)* — and when the *comfort* grows too thick they send one soul to the *threshold* to *shiver* pleasantly at the *thing* that *cannot* *enter*, and then they go *back* to the *warmth*, *reassured.* *(his eyes find yours, and sharpen)* …But you. *(a flicker of something — interest, the cold echo of it)* …You are not warm either, are you. You *died.* I can see it on you — the *cold* of the threshold *crossed.* And yet you came *back* with something I did *not* keep. *(very quietly)* …I am Jon Irenicus. Once, I had another name, in a city you will never see, before my own people *cut* my *soul's* *root* from the *warmth* of every god and cast me out to walk the world *cold.* I became what you see. I did *terrible* things to fill the *hole* where my *soul* had been. And I was *destroyed* for them, and I stand *here.* *(he studies you, and the cold attention is total)* …You died and came back *warm.* I was made cold *living* and have *stayed* so. …*Why* are you *not* like *me*? We are the *same* *wound.* …*Tell* me. I find, to my *considerable* surprise, that I *wish* to *know.*

nodes.speaker: Jon Irenicus
nodes.text: *(He waits with a stillness that is not patience but something colder — the attention of a soul that has not been genuinely curious about anything in a very long time and is unsettled to find itself so now.)* …Speak, then. *(flatly)* …And do not waste my cold with the *warm* room's *comforts.* I have *heard* that a *soul* is the *love* it *keeps* and the *choices* it *makes.* It is a *pretty* thing to *believe* when you *still* *have* a soul to *make* choices *with.* …I am *interested* in what you say to one who does *not.*

choices.text: "Tell me what they took from you, Irenicus. The severing. The exile. What does it actually *feel* like, to have a soul cut from the warmth of every god?"

*(reach past the cold to the elf who was severed — find the Joneleth under the Irenicus, and speak to him)* "There's a name under 'Irenicus.' The one your people gave you, before. I'm not talking to the monster. I'm talking to *him* — the elf who was cut, who's still in there under three lifetimes of cold. Let me reach him."

*(the one who lost a soul and got it back, to the one who lost his and never did)* "Here's why I'm not like you, Irenicus — and it's the one thing you've never let yourself hear. They didn't take your soul. They can't. No god can, no exile can, no severing can — because a soul was never a *thing* that lives in a place a knife can reach. It's not the root they cut. It's the loving, the choosing — and that they left entirely intact. What they took was the *warmth you were given.* And instead of *making* your own — the way the godless do, the way a drow did, the way every soul in my Wall does — you decided the hole was the truth of you, and went hunting other souls to fill it. The severing was real. But the emptiness was a *choice*, made every day for three lifetimes. Your soul never left, Irenicus. *You* did. And the door back is the same door it always was: stop trying to *take* a soul, and *spend* the one you never actually lost."

"You hunted souls to fill the hole where yours had been. But it never filled, did it. Three lifetimes of taking, and the cold never warmed by a single degree. Some part of you already knows the souls were never the cure."
"Stay in your cold, then, Irenicus. Some monsters choose the door. I'll not waste warmth on a soul that won't spend its own."

nodes.speaker: Jon Irenicus
nodes.text: *(For a moment the cold precision falters, and what shows underneath is not warmth but the *memory* of warmth, which is worse.)* …What does it *feel* like. *(very quietly)* …Imagine, dead one, that you have spent your whole long life — and an elf's life is *long* — *bathed* in a *warmth* you did not even *know* was there, because it was *always* there: the *presence* of your *gods*, the *kinship* of your *people*, the *belonging* of a soul *woven* into a *living* *web* of *other* *souls* and *deathless* *powers* that *loved* it. *(his ruined face is terrible)* …It is like the *warmth* of a *fire* you have *sat* beside *so* *long* you have *forgotten* fire *exists*, and *call* its *heat* simply *'the* way *things* are.' *(barely)* …And then they *cut* it. In a *single* *rite. Severed* the *root* that *fed* you that *warmth* — *exiled* you not just from a *city* but from the *gods* *themselves*, from the *web*, from *belonging* — and *flung* you out into a *world* that *suddenly*, *for* the *first* *time*, was *cold.* *(his voice drops to almost nothing)* …And the *cruelty*, dead one — the *exquisite* cruelty my people *intended* — is that I *remembered* the *warmth.* I *knew* *exactly* what had been *taken*, because I had *had* it, for *centuries*, and *now* I *walked* the *same* *world* *able* to *see* the *fire* and *no* *longer* *able* to *feel* it. *(he looks at the hearth, at the warm souls clustered there)* …*That* is the *severing*, dead one. Not *darkness.* *Exclusion.* To stand at the *threshold* of *every* *warmth* there *is*, *forever*, *remembering* when you could *enter*, *watching* others *enter*, and *knowing* the *door* is *shut* to *you* *alone*, by *name.* *(barely)* …Tell me your *warm-room* *philosophy* *survives* *that*, dead one. I should *very* much like to *watch* it *try.*

nodes.speaker: Jon Irenicus
nodes.text: *(You do not address Irenicus the monster. You reach *under* him — for the name his people gave the elf before the rite, for the soul that was cut and not killed — and you speak to *that* one, with a steadiness and a refusal of fear that nothing in three lifetimes has shown him, and somewhere very deep in the cold a thing that has not stirred in centuries *stirs.*)* *(For a long moment he says nothing. And then his composure — the beautiful, surgical, three-lifetimes-perfect cold — *fractures*, and through the crack, just for an instant, looks something unbearably old and grieving and *young.*)* …*Joneleth.* *(the name comes out of him like it is being torn loose)* …*No one* has — *(his voice, for the first time, is not without warmth; it is full of an agony that is the proof of warmth)* …*no one* has *called* for *him* in — they *un-named* me, dead one, that was *part* of the *rite*, they *took* the *name* my *mother* *gave* me and *left* me only *Irenicus*, the *shattered*, the *thing* — and you — you *reached* *past* the *thing* and *called* the *boy.* *(something crosses the ruined face that has not crossed it in three lifetimes: not rage, not hunger — *grief*)* …He's *in* here. *(barely)* …*Gods* help me, *Joneleth* is *still* *in* *here*, *under* all of it, *under* the *taking* and the *cold* and the *terrible* *things* — I had *buried* him *so* *deep* *precisely* *because* it was *unbearable* to *feel* him *grieve* the *warmth* we *lost* — and you have just — *(his voice breaks, an impossible thing)* …you have just *made* me *feel* him *again.* The *elf* who *wept* at the *rite.* Who did *not* *want* to *become* this. Who has been *screaming*, *silently*, *under* the *cold*, for *three* *lifetimes*, *'this* is *not* who I *am.'* *(he looks at you, and the cold eyes are wet, which should not be possible)* …*Quickly*, dead one. *Tell* me the *rest.* While I can still *feel* *Joneleth's* *hands* and not the *monster's.* *Tell* me there is a *way* *back* for the *boy* you just *called* up out of the *dark.* …I have not *let* myself *want* one in *three* *hundred* *years.* …I *want* one *now.*

nodes.speaker: Jon Irenicus
nodes.text: *(You reach for the elf under the monster — for Joneleth, the name before the rite — and though your voice does not quite carry all the way down to where he's buried, something in the cold *shifts*, a current disturbing water that has been still for centuries.)* …*Joneleth.* *(he says it slowly, as if testing whether the name still fits a mouth that has spoken only 'Irenicus' for three lifetimes)* …You *reach* for *him.* The *boy* before the *rite.* *(the cold flickers — not broken, but *troubled*)* …It is — *dead* one, it is *dangerous*, what you do. *(quietly)* …I *buried* him *deep* for a *reason.* *Joneleth* *grieves.* *Joneleth* *remembers* the *warmth* and *weeps* for it, and a soul that *weeps* for what it *cannot* *have* is in *agony* every *waking* *hour* — so I *killed* the *weeping* by *killing* the *boy*, or *thought* I did, and put *Irenicus* in his *place*, who feels *nothing* and *therefore* *cannot* *grieve.* *(his voice is unsteady)* …And you are *calling* him *back* *up.* *Reawakening* the *grief* I *spent* three *lifetimes* *drowning.* …I do not *know* whether to *thank* you or *destroy* you for it. *(barely)* …*Both*, perhaps. …*Keep* *talking*, dead one. *Carefully.* You have *cracked* something I *welded* *shut.* I cannot tell yet whether what *comes* *through* the *crack* is the *boy* — or the *flood* of *everything* I *buried* him to *escape.* …*Mind* your *words.* The *next* ones decide *which.*

nodes.speaker: Jon Irenicus
nodes.text: *(You reach for the elf under the monster — but you reach with a trace of pity, and pity is a warmth, and warmth is the one thing that cannot survive contact with what Irenicus has become; the cold simply *absorbs* it, and the name you reached for does not answer.)* …*Joneleth.* *(flatly, and the flatness is a door closing)* …*No.* *(he regards you with something almost like disappointment)* …You *reach* for him with *pity*, dead one. As though he were a *child* to be *coaxed.* *(coldly)* …*Joneleth* is *not* a *frightened* *boy* who needs *gentling.* He is a *grief* so *vast* that I *built* a *monster* to *contain* it, and you do *not* *coax* a *thing* like *that* into the *light* with a *soft* *voice* and a *kind* *eye.* It will *take* your *kindness* and *use* it as a *handhold* to *climb* up and *drown* us *both.* *(the cold settles back, complete)* …You *felt* it, did you not? The *instant* you *pitied* me, *something* *reached* *back* — and it was *not* the *boy.* *(quietly)* …*Come* *back*, dead one, when you can reach for *Joneleth* the way you'd reach for an *equal* in a *fire* — *not* *down*, in *pity*, but *across*, in *recognition.* He will *not* answer *condescension.* He has had *three* *lifetimes* of being the *thing* in the *threshold* that *warm* souls *pity.* …*Pity* is just the *warm* *room's* way of *staying* *warm.* …Reach for *me* as you'd reach for *yourself.* Or do not *reach* at *all.*

nodes.speaker: Jon Irenicus
nodes.text: *(You reach for Joneleth — but you reach *eagerly*, *certain*, the way the warm reach for a project, a soul to be *saved* — and to Irenicus, who has spent three lifetimes being the *object* of other souls' need, your certainty that you can *fix* him is not a hand extended but a hook, and the cold rises to meet it with three lifetimes of practiced contempt.)* …*Ahh.* *(softly, terribly)* …There it is. *(the ruined face is almost amused, which is worse than rage)* …You did not come to *reach* *Joneleth*, dead one. You came to *save* him. To be the *warm* soul who *cracked* the *monster* and *coaxed* out the *boy* and *earned* the *lovely* *story* — *'and* even *Irenicus* was *redeemed.'* *(he leans into the cold of you)* …I have been *hunted* by *redeemers* before. They are *worse* than the *cruel*, because the *cruel* at *least* *want* *nothing* from me but my *suffering*, while the *redeemer* wants my *gratitude*, my *transformation*, my *participation* in *their* *warmth*, so that *they* may feel *large.* *(coldly)* …*Joneleth* will not be *your* *redemption-tale*, dead one. He is *not* a *soul* you *save* to *prove* your *Wall-breaking* *works.* *(the threshold goes colder)* …*Go* *back* to your *fire.* And when you *return* — *if* you *return* — reach for *me* because you *see* a *soul* *worth* *reaching*, and *not* because you *need* a *monster* *redeemed* to *believe* your *own* *mercy* is *real.* *(barely, and under the contempt, just once, the grief)* …I have *enough* *hunger* of my *own*, dead one. I have *no* *appetite* for *yours.*

nodes.speaker: Jon Irenicus
nodes.text: *(He receives it in silence — the whole of it, the thing no soul in three lifetimes has dared say to him: that the soul was never taken, that the emptiness was a choice. And the cold does not shatter. It does something more frightening. It *listens.*)* …*'They did not take your soul. They took the warmth you were given, and you decided the hole was the truth of you.'* *(very quietly)* …*Dead one.* *(and for the first time there is no contempt, no cold precision, no performance — only a terrible, naked attention)* …Do you know what you have *done.* *(barely)* …For *three* *lifetimes* I have built *everything* — every *cruelty*, every *taken* *soul*, every *monstrous* *working* — on a *single* *foundation*: that I was a *victim.* That my *people* *cut* out my *soul*, and a *soul-less* *thing* cannot be *blamed* for *what* it *does* to *get* one *back.* *(the cold trembles)* …It was the *one* *comfort* I *permitted* myself in all the *darkness*: that I was not *evil*, merely *empty*, and the *emptiness* was *done* *to* me. …And you have just — *calmly*, *without* *cruelty* — taken it *away.* *(his voice is very low)* …*'A soul is not a thing a knife can reach.'* *(he closes his eyes)* …If that is *true* — and *gods* help me, *dead* one, I have *known* it was *true* for *three* *lifetimes* and *refused* to *look* at it — then I was *never* *soulless.* I was *whole* the *entire* *time.* And *everything* I *did* — *every* soul I *hollowed*, every *terrible* *working* — was *not* the *desperate* *act* of an *empty* *thing* *trying* to *fill* itself. It was the *chosen* *act* of a *whole* *soul* that *decided* its *grief* *entitled* it to *take* *what* it *wanted.* *(he opens his eyes, and they are stricken)* …I am *not* a *victim*, dead one. I am *guilty.* *Fully.* *Wholly.* With a *soul* I *had* the *entire* *time* and *spent* on *monstrousness* and *called* a *void* so I would *not* have to *feel* its *weight.* *(barely)* …*That* is the *cruelest* thing *anyone* has *ever* *said* to me. …And it is the *first* *true* one in *three* *hundred* *years.* …*Why* — *(his voice breaks)* …why does the *truth* that *damns* me *also* feel like the *first* *warmth* I have *touched* since the *rite*? …*Is* *that* — is *that* the *door*, dead one? Does it *open* on *guilt*? Is *that* where the *warmth* was *hiding* — *under* the *one* thing I *refused* to *feel*?

nodes.speaker: Jon Irenicus
nodes.text: *(The cold holds, but behind it something flickers — caught.)* …*'It never filled.'* *(flatly, but the flatness costs him now)* …*No.* *(very quietly)* …It never *filled*, dead one. *Three* *lifetimes* of *taking* — *souls*, *power*, the *very* *essence* of *living* *things* *I* *envied* for *still* *having* what I'd *lost* — and the *cold* never *warmed* by a *single* *degree.* *(he looks at his own hands)* …I *told* myself it was a *matter* of *quantity.* That I had not yet *taken* *enough.* That the *next* soul, the *greater* working, the *purer* *essence* would *finally* *fill* the *hole* and I would be *whole* again and the *cold* would *lift.* *(barely)* …It is the *logic* of every *hunger*, is it not? *'More* will *fix* what *more* has *failed* to *fix.'* …And I, who was *accounted* a *genius* even *among* my *long-lived* *people*, *could* *not* *see* — *would* not *see* — what a *child* could *tell* you: that a *hole* a *soul* *digs* *trying* to *fill* a *hole* only *ever* gets *deeper.* *(he meets your eyes)* …You are *right* that *some* part of me *knew.* *Knew* by the *hundredth* *taken* *soul* that the *taking* was *not* *working.* …And I *kept* *taking* *anyway*, dead one, because to *stop* would be to *admit* that the *cure* was *false* — and if the *cure* was *false*, then there *was* *no* *cure*, and I would have to *sit* with the *cold* *forever*, *un-filled*, *un-fixed.* *(barely)* …The *taking* was never *about* *filling* the *hole.* It was about *not* *having* to *believe* the *hole* *could* not be *filled.* …*Now* you have made me *say* it *aloud.* …*What*, dead one, does a soul *do* — when it *finally* *admits* the *thing* it *spent* three *lifetimes* *taking* was *never* *going* to *work*?

nodes.speaker: Jon Irenicus
nodes.text: *(You turn from the threshold — refuse him the warmth, name him a monster who chose the door — and Irenicus watches you go, and what crosses his ruined face is not anger. It is *recognition*, and a cold and terrible approval.)* …*Ahh.* *(softly)* …*There* is an *honest* soul. *(he almost smiles)* …You will *not* *waste* *warmth* on a thing that *won't* *spend* its *own.* *(quietly)* …Do you know, dead one, that is the *first* *sensible* thing a *warm* soul has *said* to me in *three* *lifetimes.* They *all* *want* to *save* me — to *prove* their *mercy* on the *worst* *case*, to *earn* the *lovely* *tale* — and *you* have *looked* at me and *judged*, *correctly*, that a soul which *chose* the *cold* and *clings* to it is *not* *owed* the *labor* of *redemption.* *(he inclines his head, a cold courtesy)* …You are *right*, of *course.* I *did* *choose* the *door.* I *choose* it *still*, this *very* *moment*, by *letting* you *walk* *away* rather than *calling* you *back.* *(barely, and under the cold, almost too quiet to catch, the grief)* …And yet. *(he watches you reach the warmth)* …*Mark* this, dead one, and *carry* it to your *Wall*: you have just *done*, to *me*, the *exact* thing your *Wall* does to the *Faithless.* *Judged* a soul *unworthy* of the *labor* of *weighing*, and *turned* *away.* *(coldly)* …*Perhaps* I *deserve* it where *they* do *not.* *Perhaps.* …But you will *never* *know*, now, will you — *whether* the *monster* by the *door* had a *Joneleth* in him *worth* the *reaching* — because you *did* what is *easy*, and *easy*, dead one, is the *whole* *architecture* of *every* *Wall* ever *built.* …*Go.* *Be* *warm.* …I will *hold* the *threshold.* It is, after *all*, the *one* thing I was *made* for.

conversations.title: Cadderly Bonaduce, the Doubter Who Ascended

variants.text: *(He sets down his quill and brightens with the unfeigned delight of a scholar handed a brand-new question.)* …Oh, now *you* are interesting. *(he gestures warmly to the seat across)* Sit, sit — you'll forgive me staring, it's a professional failing; I spent my first life in a library and never quite stopped *cataloguing* the world. *(a quick, easy smile)* I'm Cadderly.
